coredata.dc:title: One-pot solvothermal preparation of triple-emission N, Cl doped carbon quantum dots from waste traditional Chinese medicines as a fluorescent sensor for sensing water and Cr (Ⅵ)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

Mao, Yanpeng

Song, Tinglu
Song, Zhanlong
Zhao, Xiqiang
Wang, Wenlong
coredata.dc:description: In this research, N, Cl doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with triple-fluorescence centers was prepared by one-pot solvothermal method from waste traditional Chinese medicines as the raw material. The obtained N, Cl doped CQDs showed purple, yellow and red fluorescence under the 326, 380 and 578 nm excitation, respectively, of which red fluorescence was induced by the increase of concentrations and sensed rapidly water contents in 30–100% range with a limited of detection (LOD) of 0.65%. The obtained CQDs was applied for sensing Cr (Ⅵ) by triple channels, which exhibited the high selectivity and sensitivity for Cr (Ⅵ) with the LOD of 0.28, 0.87 and 0.90 μM, respectively. And its derivative materials exhibited the good linear range with 10–100 μM and 5–250 μM taking purple fluorescence as the response signal, respectively. The recoveries of N, Cl doped CQDs in tap water and sludge were in 93–110% range, suggesting a feasible avenue for accurate and rapid sensing of Cr (Ⅵ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The 2D fluorescence spectra (a-f) and photograph at 365 nm excitation (g) of CQDs in the different solvents (The solvents were water, DMF, HAc, n-butanol, MeOH and ethanol from left to right and top to bottom, respectively). The (x, y, z) was explained where x, y and z represented the excitation, emission wavelength and fluorescence intensity, respectively.

TEM image (inset SAED pattern and HRTEM image) (a) and size distribution of N, Cl doped CQDs (b), the SEM images of ZIF-8 (c), N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 (d), PVA film (e), N, Cl doped CQDs@PVA film (f), and FT-IR spectrum of N, Cl doped CQDs, ZIF-8 and N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 (g).
Survey (a), C1s (b), O1s and N1s (d) XPS spectra of the N, Cl doped CQDs.

Optical characterizations and stability of N, Cl doped CQDs. 3D fluorescence spectrum (a), UV-Vis absorption spectra of Cr (Ⅵ), N, Cl doped CQDs and the mixture (inset the photograph of N, Cl doped CQDs under 365 nm light, b), the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl doped CQDs under different NaCl solutions (c) and irradiation time (d).
Red fluorescence spectrum (a) and fluorescence intensity with different water contents (inset the linear relationship between intensity and water contents, b).

Optimization of the sensing parameters including the concentrations of N, Cl doped CQDs (a) and reaction time (b), the fluorescence intensity changes of N, Cl doped CQDs varying with the different concentrations of Cr (Ⅵ) under excitation wavelength of 326 nm, 380 nm and 578 nm (c), the linear relationship between the changes of fluorescence intensity and the different concentrations of Cr (Ⅵ) under 326, 380 and 578 nm excitation, respectively (d-f).

The sensitivity of sensing Cr (Ⅵ) for solid phase materials. The fluorescence spectrum of N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 at excitation of 256 nm under different Cr (Ⅵ) concentrations (a) (inset the photographs of N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 (on top left of the photographs) and the mixture of N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 and Cr (Ⅵ) (on bottom right of the photographs) under sun light (1) and 365 nm excitation (2), respectively), the relationship between fluorescence changes of N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8, F/F 0, and Cr (Ⅵ) concentrations (b), the fluorescence spectrum of N, Cl doped CQDs@PVA at excitation of 250 nm under different Cr (Ⅵ) concentrations (c) (inset the photographs of N, Cl doped CQDs@PVA film sensing Cr (Ⅵ) under 365 nm excitation), the relationship between fluorescence changes of N, Cl doped CQDs@PVA, F/F 0, and Cr (Ⅵ) concentrations (d).
The analysis results for sensing Cr (Ⅵ) in real samples under the excitation wavelength of 326 nm (n = 3).
The analysis results for sensing Cr (Ⅵ) in real samples under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm (n = 3).
The analysis results for sensing Cr (Ⅵ) in real samples under the excitation wavelength of 578 nm (n = 3).

head.ce:title: One-pot solvothermal preparation of triple-emission N, Cl doped carbon quantum dots from waste traditional Chinese medicines as a fluorescent sensor for sensing water and Cr (Ⅵ)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, N, Cl doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with triple-fluorescence centers was prepared by one-pot solvothermal method from waste traditional Chinese medicines as the raw material. The obtained N, Cl doped CQDs showed purple, yellow and red fluorescence under the 326, 380 and 578 nm excitation, respectively, of which red fluorescence was induced by the increase of concentrations and sensed rapidly water contents in 30–100% range with a limited of detection (LOD) of 0.65%. The obtained CQDs was applied for sensing Cr (Ⅵ) by triple channels, which exhibited the high selectivity and sensitivity for Cr (Ⅵ) with the LOD of 0.28, 0.87 and 0.90 μM, respectively. And its derivative materials exhibited the good linear range with 10–100 μM and 5–250 μM taking purple fluorescence as the response signal, respectively. The recoveries of N, Cl doped CQDs in tap water and sludge were in 93–110% range, suggesting a feasible avenue for accurate and rapid sensing of Cr (Ⅵ).

ce:keyword.ce:text: the mix of Ligusticum wallichii and Corydalis
Thermogravimetric analysis and differential scanning calorimeter
Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8, ZIF-8 encapsulating N, Cl doped CQDs
Cl doped CQDs@PVA, PVA film encapsulating N, Cl doped CQDs

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, traditional Chinese medicines (TCM) have been widely attracted in the world because they have the immense potentials in treating difficult diseases such as cancers, malaria, Corona Virus Disease 2019 and so on[1,2]. With the gradual development of TCM, lots of waste TCM are generated, for instance, about 30 million tons of waste TCM are produced every year in China [3]. However, current treatment methods for waste TCM such as landfilling, incineration and stacking might cause the waste of resources and pollution of environment considering the treatment cost and economic gains. The abundant cellulose, lignin, hemicellulose and some active ingredients in waste TCM are wasted, causing the waste of resources. And waste TCM have the advantages of simple availability, large production, rich functional groups and abundant impurities. Therefore, the recycling and utilization of organic substances in waste TCM have become an interesting research hotpot. Researchers turned them into bioethanol [4,5], animal feed [6,7]and carbon materials [8,9] through biological or thermochemical technologies. Nevertheless, existing biotransformation technologies exhibit the drawbacks of low energy efficiency, high cost and difficulty in storage. The conversion of waste TCM into carbon materials by thermochemical methods avoids the above problems and enhances the high added value of waste biomass. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a novel fluorescent nanomaterial, has the excellent optical characterizations and good water solubility. However, few of literatures reported CQDs was prepared from waste TCM. Li et al. [10] prepared blue-fluorescence CQDs without any surface passivation and modification from mulberry leaves residues as raw materials, which could emit strong fluorescence for intracellular bioimaging and drug delivery, but the blue fluorescence may cause the irreversible injury for biology. Therefore, the way of preparing CQDs from waste TCM as precursors provides a feasible thought to enhance the added value and reduce the environmental contaminations.

In recent years, CQDs depending on the advantages of tunable photoluminescence, excellent water solubility, extraordinary biocompability and simple synthesis method, is attracted incredible attention which is widely applied in sensing[11–13], photocatalysis[14–16], light emitted diode [17,18], anti-counterfeiting ink [19,20] and biology fields [21–23]. It is well known that the types of precursors (for example small organic compounds, chemical reagents and nature substances) and preparation methods (for instance pyrolysis, hydrothermal method, chemical oxidation and laser ablation) have the significant influence on the physical characterizations and optical features. Waste biomasses show the characterizations of simple accessibility, abundant impurities and rich functional groups. Hitherto, many waste biomasses are used to prepare the CQDs through thermochemical methods, however, the obtained CQDs generally emits blue or green fluorescence [24–26]. Therefore, the synthesis of multiple-color fluorescence from biomasses as the precursors is becoming a tendency. Desai et al. [27] reported the CQDs with blue, green and yellow fluorescence from muskmelon fruit by different kinds of acid oxidation. Tang et al. [28] reported dual-emission biomass CQDs with near infrared fluorescence from black pepper as the raw materials was prepared by different heat treatment means and applied in sensing Fe3+. Wu et al. [29] studied dual-emission and single-emission CQDs using fresh leek were applied in sensing Cu2+ and tetracycline, but the synthesis of multiple-color CQDs must process many treatment steps. Park et al. [30] utilized waste paper to synthesize CQDs with blue, green and yellow fluorescence, following that the CQDs was encapsulated into PVA film for anti-counterfeiting application. From the perspectives of preparation of multiple-color CQDs, large of studies reported that the methods adopted the different precursors or treatments, but they had the disadvantages of prolix operation steps, reaction time-consuming and requirements for more organic solvent comparing with one-pot solvothermal method. Besides, the multiple-emission CQDs is mainly derived from chemical reagents in the current reports, therefore, it always the request for researchers that CQDs is prepared from natural, obtained easily and environmental friendly carbon resources.

With the development of modern industries such as electroplating, metallurgy and paint, chromium compounds are widely applied resulting in the serious pollution. Chromium exists in nature as trivalent and hexavalent, and hexavalent toxic is ca. 100 times more than that of Cr (Ⅲ) [31]. Excessive hexavalent chromium could enrich along with food chains leading to difficult elimination and have genotoxic and carcinogenic due to its high oxidation potential and ability to penetrate biomembranes. Therefore, quantitative detection of Cr (Ⅵ) in environment is extremely essential. At present, the methods for detecting Cr (Ⅵ) include ion chromatography[32], colorimetric[33], atomic absorption spectrophotometry [34]and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS)[35]. However, these detecting technologies have the shortcomings of tedious operation, low sensitivity and time consuming which would obstruct the development of rapid and visual detection. With the development of fluorescence technologies, they are considered to avoid the above problems and exhibit the advantages of rapid sensing, real time and intuitive process. Furthermore, CQDs with low toxicity, good water solubility and excellent photobleaching resistance, stands out in the fluorescence technologies. CQDs was widely applied in sensing Cr (Ⅵ) in many literatures. Yuan et al. [36] studied the cellulose nanofibers with lignin-based CQDs whose absorption capacity was 599.9 mg g-1 for Cr (Ⅵ) and limited of detection (LOD) was 11.2 mg L-1. Bhatt et al. [37]synthesized nitrogen doped CQDs from cheap prickly pear through microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, which could detect the Cr (Ⅵ) with a LOD of 0.04 mM through inner filter effect and draw fluorescent patterns. In the process of fluorescent detection using CQDs, the most common method was single-channel detection. However, when single channel was used to detect metal ions, the processes were readily disturbed by other substances, following that the accuracy and stability of the sensor would decrease largely. Multiple detection channels could extend the detecting range and enhance the detecting robust. Furthermore, the fluorescent devices were extremely dependent while sensing heavy metal ions by the CQDs. It could not cope with some special and difficult environments. Therefore, the development of fluorescent materials for rapid and visual detection has been attention.

In this work, N, Cl doped CQDs from waste the mixture of Ligusticum wallichii and Corydalis (waste LWC) via one-pot solvothermal method was prepared by optimizing the reaction solvent, temperature, time and pH value to realize the recycling of waste biomass and goal of synthesizing multiple-emission centers from waste biomass. The unique optical characterizations of triple-emission CQDs were measured and the synthesized fluorescence intensity was tunable through different concentrations. The triple channels of N, Cl doped CQDs accomplished the sensitive and accurate sensing of Cr (Ⅵ). To ensure the rapid, visual and convenient detection, its derivative materials, zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film encapsulating N, Cl doped CQDs, further were used to sense Cr (Ⅵ). In order to the practicability of analysis method, the real tests were accomplished in tap water and sludge. The fluorescence sensor based on N, Cl doped CQDs with triple emissions provides an effective, sensitive and accurate strategy for detection of Cr (Ⅵ).

Waste TCM was the mixture of waste Ligusticum wallichii and Corydalis (waste LWC) which was obtained from Shandong Freda Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Jinan, China). Polyvinyl alcohol 1799 (98–99% (mol/mol), PVA), AlCl3 (AR, 99.0%), CrCl3·6 H2O (AR, 98.5%), MnCl2·4 H2O (AR, 99.0%), NiCl2·6 H2O (AR, 99.0%), n-Butanol (AR, 99.0%), L-Ascorbic acid (BR, 99.0%, AA) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). O-phenylenediamine (CP, 98.5%, OPD), ethanol (AR, 99.7%), hydrochloric acid (AR, 36.0–38.0%, HCl), acetic acid glacial (AR, 99.5%, HAc), methanol (AR, 99.5%, MeOH), N, N-dimethylformamide (AR, 99.5%, DMF), KNO3 (AR, 99.0%), NaCl (AR, 99.5%), Ca(NO3)2·4 H2O (AR, 99.0%), FeCl3·6 H2O (AR, 99.0%), FeCl2·4 H2O (AR, 98.0%), CoCl2·6 H2O (AR, 99.0%), Cu(NO3)2·3 H2O (AR, 99.0%), L-histidine (BR, 98.5%, His), glycine (BR, 99.0%, Gly), L-lysine (BR, 98.5%, Lys), sucrose (BR, 99.0%, Suc), L-glutamate (BR, 98.5%, Glu), DL-tartaric acid (BR, 99.0%, Tar), D(-)-fructose (BR, 98.0%, Fru) were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Zn(NO3)2·6 H2O (AR, 99.0%), 2-methylimidazole (98%, Hmim), K2CrO4 (AR, 99.0%) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, Cl doped CQDs and its derivative materials
ce:section.ce:para: N, Cl doped CQDs was prepared by an improved one-pot solvothermal method involved from the literature [38]. Specifically, 0.040 g of waste LWC and 0.300 g of OPD were added into 40 mL ethanol solution and were mixed by ultrasound lasting for 15 min firstly. And then 0.25 mL of HCl was added into the above mixture and stirred. Subsequently, the solution was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 9 h. After natural cooling, the reaction suspension was collected by 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove large particles, the supernate was transferred into the dialysis bag (molecular weight cut off =1000 Da) and was dialyzed for 24 h to remove the unreacted substances and impurities. Finally, the solid of N, Cl doped CQDs was obtained by rotary evaporation at the temperature of 60 ℃ under low pressure.

The ZIF-8 was prepared using the universal method with slight modifications [39]. A solution and B solution were obtained by dissolving 3.570 g of Zn(NO3)2 6 H2O and 7.883 g Hmim into 120 mL MeOH solution, respectively. Afterward, A solution was poured tardily into B solution under constantly stirring for 1 h to obtain the milky suspension, then the pure ZIF-8 was obtained by washing with MeOH solution and drying under vacuum at 65 ℃ for 24 h. Different volumes of N, Cl doped CQDs (10, 20 and 30 mL) were added into A solution to produce N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8-X, X representing the volumes of N, Cl doped CQDs, and the optimum composite material was nominated as N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8.

N, Cl doped CQDs@PVA film was prepared using a straightforward approach [40]. Briefly, 3.0 g PVA and 30 mL deionized water were stirred magnetically at 95 ℃ for 1 h. And then added N, Cl doped CQDs (10, 15 and 20 mL) into the above mixture under constantly stirring for 30 min and then transferred to a 90 mm petri dish to be horizontally dried 65 ℃ for overnight until forming a uniform and smooth film, named N, Cl doped CQDs@PVA-X, X representing the volume of N, Cl doped CQDs. Ultimately, the optimum derivative material was marked as N, Cl doped CQDs@PVA film.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM): SEM (Zeiss Supra 55, Germany) was used to observe the morphology of samples under 5 kV operating voltage, the sample was pretreated to coat gold layer for 240 s before observing. To clearly observe the morphology of the sample less than 0.2 µm, TEM (FEI Tecnai F20, USA) was a good method, and the sample was coated on the ultrathin carbon film before characterizing the sample. High resolution TEM (HRTEM) further was characterized the lattice fringe, and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) was analyzed the distance from diffractive ring to center to ensure the type of crystal plane, furthermore the phase.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern: XRD pattern of samples was collected by powder X-ray diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical, Netherlands) under 40 kV operating voltage and 15 mA working current, whose scanning range was 5°− 70° at a speed of 0.8° min−1. The sample disk was rotated at a speed of 1 rps to obtain accurate diffraction data.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS): The surface function groups were measured by FT-IR spectrometry (Thermo Nicolet 6700, USA). The samples and KBr were uniformly ground at a mass ratio of 1:200, then was made into a disc for further testing. The scanning range was 400–4000 cm−1 with resolution of 4 cm−1 and 16 scans. XPS spectra (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) were collected using Al Kα ray under working voltage of 12 kV and filament current of 6 mA. They analyzed the surface elements of N, Cl doped CQDs and ensured whether the N and Cl were doped into CQDs successfully.

Thermogravimetric analysis and differential scanning calorimeter (TG-DSC): The thermal stability of composite materials encapsulating N, Cl doped CQDs was characterized by TG-DSC (Mettler Toledo). The heating rate was 10 ℃ min−1, the temperature range was 30–1000 ℃, and the N2 flow was 50 mL min−1.

The optical properties of CQDs were characterized by UV–visible (UV-Vis) spectra and fluorescence spectrum. The absorbance spectra of the samples were recorded by UV–Vis spectrometer (MAPADA P5, China) in ultrapure water at a range of 200–700 nm. The fluorescence spectrum was measured in ethanol solution under room temperature and ordinary pressure by fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4700, Japan). The experimental conditions of measuring N, Cl doped CQDs were under the excitation voltage of 400 V and slit width of 10 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water by red fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly speaking, N, Cl doped CQDs was dissolved into the mixed solutions of ethanol and water with different ratios. The mixture was transferred into the cuvette and recorded the fluorescence intensity at 578 nm excitation wavelength, stablishing the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and the volume percentages of water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N, Cl doped CQDs and its derivative materials for detection of Cr (Ⅵ)
ce:section.ce:para: When measuring Cr (Ⅵ) by N, Cl doped CQDs, the fluorescence was measured under the conditions of excitation and emission slit width at 10 nm at ambient environment. In liquid phase of sensing Cr (Ⅵ), the procedure was performed as followed: 0.5 mL of various concentrations of Cr (Ⅵ) was added into 4 mL of N, Cl doped CQDs solution (0.6 mg mL−1) incubating for 8 min and then measured fluorescence intensity.

For solid phase detection, 200 μL ions solution was added into 4 mL suspension of N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 (1.0 mg mL−1) and incubated for 8 min to evaluate the fluorescence intensity. The intensity of mixture was measured under 256 nm excitation at 5 nm slit width. While N, Cl doped CQDs@PVA film measured Cr (Ⅵ), the film was placed into various concentrations of Cr (Ⅵ) lasting for 8 min. Recorded the fluorescence intensity and spectra of film after drying them under the conditions of 250 nm excitation at 10 nm slit width. For naked-eye colorimetric assays, the corresponding photos were shoot by a mobile phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real tests for sensing Cr (Ⅵ) in tap water and sludge
ce:section.ce:para: The application of sensing Cr (Ⅵ) was implemented in tap water and sludge. Tap water was collected from the laboratory in Shandong University. Before using the water, the residual chlorine was eliminated by boiling it for 10 min. Sludge was obtained from Shandong Runde Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Taian, Shandong). Primarily, the collected sludge was dried and ground to less than 100 meshes. Accurately weighed of 0.1 g sludge powder and placed it into Teflon digestion tanks, then added 1 mL H2O2, 8 mL aqua regia and 2 mL hydrofluoric acid in proper order and heated them at 200 ℃ for 20 min. After natural cooling, diluted the digestion solution until pH = 5.0. Regardless of tap water or digestion solution of sludge, the original concentrations of Cr (Ⅵ) were detected by ICP-MS in the real samples, further different concentrations of Cr (Ⅵ) were analyzed in real samples using N, Cl doped CQDs to calculate the recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization parameters for the preparation of N, Cl doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: First of all, the element compounds of waste LWC were analyzed, of which the heteroatom was about 3.15% as shown in Table. S1 (The relative quantum yield of CQDs was 3.58% from waste LWC using water as solvent), suggesting that preparing the CQDs from waste LWC as the precursor was feasible. To modify the internal electron structures and improve the fluorescent properties of CQDs[41,42], nitrogen and chlorine were doped into CQDs during the preparation process. The prepared CQDs converted from blue color to multiple color and the fluorescence intensity was improved ( Fig. 1). Solvent impacted prominently on the fluorescence emissions and intensity (Fig. 1). When ethanol was used as solvent, the prepared CQDs had the satisfying optical properties from the perspectives of fluorescence centers and intensity. Furthermore, the preparing parameters including reaction temperature, reaction time, the OPD mass and pH were investigated taken the fluorescence intensity as the reference at 326 nm and 578 nm excitation under the conditions of the same absorbance. The formation of carbon core depended mainly on the reaction temperature. As shown in Fig. S1a, the intensity of N, Cl doped CQDs increased from 160 ℃ to 180 ℃ and then decreased. The intensity accompanying with the increase of reaction time, exhibited the same tendency as that of temperature during 3–12 h (Fig. S1b). The strongest fluorescence intensity was obtained for 9 h. Fig. S1c displayed the purple and red fluorescence intensity both reduced with the increase of OPD. The intensity ratio of them was close to 1.0 after adding 3.0 g of OPD. HCl played a significant role in adjusting solution pH promoting the hydrolysis of biomassand enhancing the reaction rate[38]. As the same time, doping Cl into the electron structures of CQDs improved the physical and chemical properties of CQDs[41]. With decreasing pH, purple fluorescence intensity first increased and then decreased obviously, furthermore red intensity kept the inclination of decrease in Fig. S1d. Eventually, the volume of HCl was 0.25 mL considering the short wavelength and low energy of purple fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of N, Cl doped CQDs' derivative materials
ce:section.ce:para: The intensity of N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 and N, Cl doped CQDs@PVA film was affected obviously by the numbers of CQDs in Fig. S2. When increasing the volumes of N, Cl doped CQDs, purple intensity of N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 was gradually enhanced, but yellow and red intensity gradually decreased (Fig. S2a). Given the slow growth speed of purple intensity, adding 20 mL of N, Cl doped CQDs was reasonable. Fig. S2b exhibited purple and red fluorescence intensity of N, Cl doped CQDs@PVA film reached the maximum when adding 15 mL N, Cl doped CQDs solution, and yellow fluorescence intensity always reduced gently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N, Cl doped CQDs and its derivative materials
ce:section.ce:para: The micromorphology of N, Cl doped CQDs was observed by TEM and HRTEM images in Fig. 2a. One was that the particles exceeded 10 nm and maybe be impurities, and the other was called N, Cl doped CQDs which was well-dispersed spherical nanoparticles with average diameter of 1.95 nm (analyzing 200 nanoparticles). HRTEM image (the insets in Fig. 2a) exhibited a lattice fringe with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm representing the (100) crystal plane of graphite [43], which was proved by SAED pattern.

The chemical compounds and surface functional groups were identified by elemental analysis, FT-IR and XPS. In Table. S2A, the element contents of C, H, O, N and Cl were 52.50%, 5.53%, 12.67%, 18.63% and 10.67%, respectively, suggesting that Cl and N had been successfully co-doped into CQDs. The empirical formula for N, Cl doped CQDs was approximately C14H18O3N4Cl as shown in Table. S2B. Therefore, the N, Cl doped CQDs was chlorine and nitrogen functionalized. In addition, FT-IR and XPS spectra were used to conform the above results. Fig. 2g depicted the peaks at 2865 cm−1, 1624 cm−1 and 763 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of =C-H, CO and C-C [44,45]. The appearance of peaks located at 3411 cm−1, 3330 cm−1, 1526 cm−1 and 1321 cm−1 was corresponding to –NH, -OH, C-N-C and C-O. The peak at 1496 cm−1 was assigned to stretching vibration C-N or CN, and the peak at 763 cm−1 was an indication of C-Cl stretching vibration absorption suggesting the successful doping of Cl. The data of XPS spectra displayed N, Cl doped CQDs consisted of C (68.80 wt%), O (14.45 wt%), N (12.91 wt%) and Cl (3.84 wt%) in Fig. 3a, and Cl was derived from HCl. Furthermore, C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3b) analysis revealed four peaks at 284.4 eV (CC), 285.5 eV (C-N), 286.2 eV (C-O) and 287.7 eV (CN) [46,47]. O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3c) contained two bands of 531.3 eV and 532.5 eV which were attributed to CO and C-O, respectively. Moreover, N 1 s spectrum was fitted by pyridinic N (398.8 eV), pyrrolic N (400.1 eV), and amino N (401.1 eV) in Fig. 3d [38,47,48]. To summarize, the XPS results were consistent with FT-IR analysis, implying that N, Cl doped CQDs had excellent water solubility due to plentiful hydrophilic functional groups such as O-H, N-H, CO and C-O, and the doping of nitrogen and chlorine had the significant influence on carbon core and surface state.

Further, the optical characterizations of N, Cl doped CQDs were characterized. The 3D fluorescence spectrum shown in Fig. 4a revealed triple-fluorescence centers located at (326, 364), (380, 544) and (578, 646) nm, corresponding to purple, yellow and red color, respectively. It was practicable that triple-emission CQDs was prepared from waste LWC and OPD as the raw materials, but seldom researches reported the work about multiple-fluorescence CQDs from waste biomasses. It was explored that the distinctive behavior of triple emissions was co-produced from waste LWC and OPD ( Fig. S3). The CQDs from waste LWC behaved indigo color under UV light (Fig. S3b and e), the CQDs from OPD displayed red color under 365 nm excitation (Fig. S3c and e). Only did the waste LWC and OPD both exist in ethanol, the phenomenon of triple emissions would appear (Fig. S3d and e), conformed the unique optical properties were the result of interaction together. The fluorescence peaks occurred the red shift from 348 nm to 385 nm with excitation dependent behavior (Fig. S4 a) due to the various defects relative to O or N functional groups [49,50]. The yellow and red fluorescence of excitation independent behavior indicted the few surface defects (Fig. S4b and c) [51,52]. In UV-Vis absorbance spectrum in Fig. 4b, there were two strong absorbance peaks at 238 nm and 271 nm, because of the π-π * transition of sp2 carbon domain [53]. Moreover, other peaks at 458 nm, 578 nm and 631 nm were attributed to n-π * transition of CO or CN bonds [38]. The absorbance intensity, to some extent, depended on conjugated degree of carbon core and surface defect degree.

The stability of N, Cl doped CQDs was detected in different concentrations of NaCl and irradiation time under UV light. The triple-emission intensity of N, Cl doped CQDs still maintained above 80% in the NaCl solutions (Fig. 4c) suggesting CQDs had high fluorescence stability in the high ionic concentrations, of which red fluorescence was almost barely affected and yellow fluorescence fluctuated obviously. Similarly, even the irradiation time was 60 min under UV light, the intensity was only reduced by 3.8%. When increasing to 100 min, it remained above 80% in Fig. 4d, indicating that N, Cl doped CQDs had the pleasing resistance of photobleachability. All results implied N, Cl doped CQDs had the remarkable optical stability which would have a profound impact on in vivo sensing.

Furthermore, the impacts of N, Cl doped CQDs on the morphology of ZIF-8 and PVA were observed. The morphology of ZIF-8 was spherical particles with average diameter of 140 nm, but the N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 displayed irregular particles with average size of 270 nm in Fig. 2c and d. Despite the reason that the size increased significantly was not clearly, the results indicated that the CQDs influenced obviously on the ZIF-8. Under the same shooting conditions, the composite film was more porous and rougher than pure PVA film in Fig. 2e and f, which was benefit to absorption of other substances to the surface of film [54].

The XRD spectra of the derivative materials were characterized (Fig. S5). The diffraction peaks of N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 were well corresponding to that of pure ZIF-8 in Fig. S5a, indicating the successful preparation of N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8. It was worth noticed that carbon peaks were not observed, due to the small particles or low content of CQDs. And it was obviously seen that the there was a peak at 1624 cm−1 attributed to CO bonds, which was not displayed on the ZIF-8 in Fig. 2g. It was significant that there was a broad peak at 2θ= 19.5° in Fig. S5b, which arose from hydrogen bonding between the molecular chains of the PVA polymer [55]. The diffraction peak intensity increased causing from the interaction between CQDs particles and PVA polymer molecular chain [54,55].

The specific surface area of ZIF-8 and N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 was compared by N2 adsorption isotherm to ensure the position of CQDs in ZIF-8 shown in Fig. S6. The specific surface area and micropore volume of N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 increased to 1516 m2g−1 from 1419 m2g−1, 0.57 cm3g−1 from 0.53 cm3g−1 comparing with the pure ZIF-8 in Table. S2, respectively, proving that CQDs existed in the cavities rather than the pores. Moreover, the mechanical properties of film was explored (Fig. S7). When the volume of N, Cl doped CQDs was 15 mL, the elongation at break of composite film increased to 488.05% from 429.32% and tensile strength decreased to 38.39 MPa from 48.78 MPa comparing to the pure PVA, because of the formation of the hydrogen-bonded crosslinking network structure between the CQDs with rich hydrophilic groups and the side groups in PVA molecular chains [56].

The thermal stability of derivative materials was characterized by TG-DSC. Under the condition of the same mass loss, N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 requested higher temperature comparing with pure ZIF-8 (Fig. S8a), indicating that the CQDs was benefit to improve the stability of ZIF-8. Most mass loss of PVA took place in the range of 220–400 ℃ which was attributed to the elimination of side groups, following that water and flammable molecules were formed (Fig. S8b) [56]. And another stage was in 400–500 ℃ due to random chain breaking and cyclization. As the content N, Cl doped CQDs increased, the loss of composite film in 380–480 ℃ decreased, suggesting the adding of N, Cl doped CQDs solution enhanced effectively the thermal stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water sensing by red fluorescence of N, Cl doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Additionally, the water content in ethanol was sensed by fluorescence method taken red fluorescence intensity as the response signal. The fluorescence spectrum under different water contents was shown under 578 nm excitation in Fig. 5a, and the peaks occurred the blue shift to 640 nm from 651 nm. The fluorescence intensity was in fluctuating state in 10–30% range in Fig. 5b. Once water content was above 30%, the linear relationship between intensity and water content was established with a LOD (LOD=3σ/k (n = 11), where σ was the standard deviation for the blank solution, and k was the slope of the calibration curve) of 0.65% (the insets of Fig. 5b). As shown in Table. S4, previous reported organic small molecule probes and copper nanoclusters showed high sensitivity even comparable to the Karl Fischer method. Although the sensitivity of the sensor based on N, Cl doped CQDs was slightly inferior, it showed the superiority in linear range. Comparing with other CQDs, N, Cl doped CQDs had a wider linear range and the LOD was comparable to or better than theirs. But there was no doubt that this sensor had certain limitations, that was, N, Cl doped CQDs was not yet able to achieve accurate detection of trace water, which need to be improved in further work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N, Cl doped CQDs for detection of Cr (Ⅵ)
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the higher sensitivity and reliable detection results, the influence factors including the reaction concentrations and reaction time were explored in the process of sensing Cr (Ⅵ). It was well known that sensitivity and linear range were important evaluating parameters. The concentrations of N, Cl doped CQDs influenced significantly on the linear range. If the concentration of CQDs was too high, the linear range would be wider and the sensitivity would decrease; otherwise, the linear range would be narrow which was adverse to sense the target. When the N, Cl doped CQDs concentration was enhanced, the fluorescence intensity at 326 nm and 380 nm excitation increased initially and then decreased, and the intensity at 578 nm slowly increased first and then basically saturated in Fig. 6a. When the concentration of CQDs was 0.6 mg mL−1, the purple, yellow and red fluorescence intensity was satisfactory. It was evidently seen that the changes of intensity demonstrated their fluorescence centers were related to n-π * energy transition [52]. In other words, the number and distance between carbon nanodots particles in samples impacted the emission, instead of aggregation [57]. After adding more ethanol into samples, the non-radiative relaxation was weakened resulting in the enhancement of purple fluorescence, however, the intensity reduced when the concentration was less than 0.3 mg mL−1 indicating that the produce of purple fluorescence may be relative to π-π * transition of carbon core. On the contrary, the relative distance between CQDs particles decreased , which produced excition annihilation and resulted in the decrease of the fluorescence. Additionally, surface energy band of CQDs might overlap, and a new energy level lower than that of single CQDs was produced, thus, the red fluorescence intensity rose suddenly at the 0.3 mg mL−1 concentration. The phenomenon that the intensity of red color was saturated, was caused by the reabsorption process of CQDs [57]. Next, the time dependent curves of fluorescence intensity at 326, 380, 578 nm in response to Cr (Ⅵ) were studied (Fig. 6b). The fluorescence was quenched immediately within 5 min, and the intensity kept stable in the ensuing time.

Based on the low toxicity, extraordinary water-solubility and unique optical properties of N, Cl doped CQDs sensor, the sensing performances for Cr (Ⅵ) were evaluated by selectivity, linear range and sensitivity. As shown in Fig. S9a and b, the intensity excited by 326 nm decreased obviously in the presence of Fe (Ⅲ), but the intensity under 326 nm, 380 nm and 578 nm excitation stood unchanged basically in the presence of other interferences. However, the intensity was quenched dramatically by Cr (Ⅵ) comparing with the quenching effective of Fe (Ⅲ) for N, Cl doped CQDs. The average lifetimes were reduced from 1.31 ns to 1.28 ns, from 3.66 ns to 2.78 ns, from 3.45 ns to 3.21 ns (Fig. S10) under the excitation wavelength of 326 nm, 380 nm and 578 nm, respectively. Thus, the fluorescence quenching induced by Cr (Ⅵ) was considered to derive from the dynamic quenching dominated by collisional deactivation [58]. There were two strong absorption peaks at 257 nm and 350 nm of Cr (Ⅵ) overlapped with those of N, Cl doped CQDs (Fig. 4b), suggesting that it might suppress the electron transitions and cause extinction effect on fluorescence emission of N, Cl doped CQDs. Besides, Cr (Ⅵ) had stronger binding affinity with the hydrophilic groups decorated in the surface of N, Cl doped CQDs than other substances. The fluorescence changes of N, Cl doped CQDs were shown in Fig. 6c under different excitation wavelengths. With the increase of Cr (Ⅵ) concentrations, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased as a whole. It was worth noted that the phenomenon occurred simultaneously under triple excitations , demonstrating that the feasibility of N, Cl doped CQDs as triple channels for sensing Cr (Ⅵ). This triple-detection method could expand the linear range and enhance the sensing stability. The linear ranges were 5–350 μM, 250–600 μM, and 300–850 μM under the excitation of 320, 380 and 578 nm in Fig. 6d-f, respectively, and the corresponding LOD were 0.28 μM, 0.87 μM and 0.90 μM that expressly were both lower than the maximum level of drink water permitted by World Health Organization (WHO, 0.962 μM) [59]. These results demonstrated that the high sensitivity and accuracy of N, Cl doped CQDs were achieved by triple-channel detection of Cr (Ⅵ) in the practical environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N, Cl doped CQDs' derivative materials for detection of Cr (Ⅵ)
ce:section.ce:para: It was obviously observed that the fluorescence of N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 and N, Cl doped CQDs@PVA film was quenched by Cr (Ⅵ) under 365 nm light for naked eyes (the insets in Fig. 7a and c). The naked eyes experiments could directly observe the changes of fluorescence color which improved the sensing efficiency and were suitable for detection in the special environment and nonprofessional testing. Fig. 7a and c displayed the fluorescence spectra of N, Cl doped CQDs' derivative materials after adding different concentrations of Cr (Ⅵ). Furthermore, the linear relationship between the fluorescence changes of N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 and Cr (Ⅵ) concentrations was established in the range of 10–100 μM (the linear correlation coefficients = 0.9977), and the LOD reached to 0.83 μM while signal to noise ratio was 11 (Fig. 7b). Fig. 7d exhibited N, Cl doped CQDs @PVA film detected Cr (Ⅵ) in the linear range of 5–250 μM and with a LOD of 0.89 μM. Comparing with liquid phase sensing, the sensitivity of solid phase was lower than that of liquid phase, but, it still was lower than maximum concentration of drink water specified by the WHO [59]. Table. S5 listed the analysis method and performance analysis for Cr (Ⅵ). It was seen that, the purple fluorescence of N, Cl doped CQDs behaved the performances of the higher linear range and lower LOD. Although the LOD of yellow and red fluorescence were higher than that of listed work, they showed wider linear range. The sensing performances of N, Cl doped CQDs were equivalent to reported sensor for Cr (Ⅵ). Moreover, N, Cl doped CQDs' derivative materials also behaved the advantages of wide linear range and fast response. And their LOD were still lower than maximum concentration of drink water specified by the WHO.

As is known to all, Cr (Ⅵ) with high toxicity and oxidability poses a huge threat to human health and the ecological environment. To evaluate the proposed analysis method's application value, the real tests would be implemented in the tap water and sludge in
Table 1 to 3. Chromium element from original tap water and sludge was detected by ICP-MS, and it was lower than 0.005 μM which could be ignored. The recoveries of N, Cl doped CQDs in tap water and sludge were in 93–110% range and relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 5%. In conclusion, it was further proved that the analysis method of N, Cl doped CQDs was feasible in real samples.

In the work, triple-emission N, Cl doped CQDs was prepared from waste LWC and OPD through one-pot solvothermal method to enhance the added value of waste TCM and reduce the environmental pollution. The optical prosperities of N, Cl doped CQDs were closely related to the solvent, and it exhibited purple, yellow and red color fluorescence in ethanol solution which was turned through adjusting the concentrations and maybe be originated from the surface state. And the linear relationship between red fluorescence intensity and the water contents was established in the range of 30–100% with the LOD of 0.65%. The triple channels were quenched by Cr (Ⅵ) and the mechanism of quenching process was dominated by dynamic quenching. Besides, in order to realize the visibility and rapidity in the sensing process, N, Cl doped CQDs' derivative materials, N, Cl doped CQDs@ZIF-8 and N, Cl doped CQDs@PVA film, were utilized to detect Cr (Ⅵ) with the LOD of 0.83 μM and 0.89 μM, respectively. The recoveries were in 93–110% range in the tap water and sludge for N, Cl doped CQDs, suggesting that it will be a promising candidate for detection Cr (Ⅵ) rapidly, visually, accurately and sensitively.
